title: ASIC blasts super fund payout delays
full_text:
Superannuation trustees are still failing to handle death benefits for their grieving members on time, with the corporate regulator saying legal action remains on the table if there are no improvements.
Despite suing superannuation giants Cbus and Australian Super in recent high-profile lawsuits over delayed death benefits processing, the Australian Securities & Investments Commission is concerned the sector could be in breach of its duties to vulnerable members, according to findings in its latest compulsory survey.
Some trustees have not established simple end-to-end claims tracking – which costs nothing – ASIC commissioner Simone Constant told The Australian.
“It is worrying there are still trustees not doing that,” she said. “Trustee boards have a responsibility to their members. They need to be seeing this through the eyes of grieving members.” The regulator’s latest review of the industry reported “only modest progress on benefits claims handling times”, following a survey of 45 superannuation trustees. Only 3 per cent more death benefit cases were closed within 180 days in the year to October 31, compared to the year earlier.
Funds surveyed only reduced the number of claims open for 720 days by 2 per cent.
And, critically, these minor improvements are likely to be offset by the overall trend that claims have increased by 10 per cent in the year to October 31. Claims will continue to increase as the population ages, the report warned.
As well, despite most of the industry understanding the critical importance of monitoring end-to-end performance, 20 per cent of trustees failed to introduce performance metrics for end-to-end claims handling, according to the report.
More than 70 per cent of the trustees reviewed or committed to reviewing service agreements (which outsource some parts of claims handling, including death benefit claims handling), but the remainder stated that they didn’t think a review was necessary, or considered their existing agreements were adequate.
There had been some improvement but it was “not enough”, Ms Constant said. “It is not fast enough. It is certainly not consistent (across trustees) or comprehensive enough,” she said.
“It is worrying that there are still laggards. We (ASIC) have thrown everything at this.” The report noted “complaints about superannuation remain high”, and said trustees received more than 200,000 internal complaints in 2024-25.
“Over half of these complaints related to service issues, with delays in claim handling more broadly representing one of the top five services issues complained about,” the report said.
While large funds (managing more than $50bn) overall recorded the most improvement in the proportion of claims closed in 180 days or less (an improvement of 19 per cent), some “were significantly slower than their peers”.
Small and medium funds made less progress, and they risked falling further behind on claims handling efficiency as overall claims numbers continued to grow.
ASIC expects to release details of its latest review into advice fee deductions soon, and is likely to release findings from a further review into how trustees are using member complaints data by the end of the calendar year.
Ms Constant said: “If we see significant failings that warrants the use of (ASIC’s) full suite of regulatory tools, we absolutely will be using them. That may include writing to trustees, compliance action and enforcement actions.” ASIC’s latest report noted some “promising findings” – including a 53 per cent reduction in internal complaints about death benefit delays from early 2024 to late 2025 – but more improvements were critical, Ms Constant said in an earlier statement.
“With claims volumes increasing by 10 per in the 12 months to October 2025 and with that growth expected to continue in the context of Australia’s ageing population, it’s clear that more work needs to be done if all trustees are to meet member expectations,” she said. “We’re particularly concerned that some trustees have not actioned basic process improvements and continue exposing grieving beneficiaries to harm at times of heightened emotional and financial distress.” ASIC also called out the need for trustees to ensure they are collecting accurate data on their death benefit claims, which was highlighted in a letter sent to CEOs in November 2024.
Claims data received from trustees was simpler but several experienced challenges responding, with a number having to resubmit their data due to omissions or errors.
Cbus was fined $23.5m last year for delays processing member claims after ASIC action. ASIC’s lawsuit against AustralianSuper is ongoing, after it the nation’s biggest superannuation fund was sued last year for failing to process almost 7000 death benefit claims. Editorial P10
